speaker: interviewee
text: What you were talking about before you put that new cartridge in here is I believe I indicated that I wasn't that-- wood-- in the area where they're stored, that lumber--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --shed. And I said to myself, now how am I going to get out from here? And I knew the area like my pocket. I knew where shovels were. I knew where pickaxes were. I knew everything. Somehow I made my-- got out from under this lumber. Was careful and I watched the guards, and I timed. And they were crossing each other about every 10 minutes or 15 minutes. Besides the guard that was on the tower. So I walked over about six, eight, 10 yards to the left, and there was a [? field ?] shovel, one of those collapsible ones. And I could see. It was dark but not pitch dark. And I could see in all the good sense, and I could feel and I knew. It's because I've been in the area many, many times. And I got myself a pick axe and one of those little shovels. And as soon as the guards crossed each other and went in the opposite direction, I went to the first fence, which wouldn't be further away than 10 yards from the edge of that shed. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And I took that hand shovel in there, and I was absolutely amazed the ground was so terribly soft that it took me less than a minute to dig a little hole, a little area, that would allow me to crawl under that first set of barbed wires. And even there, I just needed to lift it 10 inches, move it up and crawl underneath. The first and the second, I don't think it to be more than three minutes to be out of the camp from the first and the second wire. The only thing that's left is that tank, anti-tank, horses, and the net that easily get caught in. The rain helped the miracle. It's like the Jews in the-- in the Sinai desert when manna rained from the-- from the sky and how [? Moshe ?] [? Rabbani ?] got the water from that rock. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And you wouldn't be surprised that I thought of those things as not religious as I am. These are those miracles that I, that you only can read in the Bible. And I neglected to mention, as I was running into the barrack before I'm tracking back now, I said to myself-- and I know these are the words that I felt, 'I knew,' quote, unquote, that's the end of the line. And I said to myself, So in God's name, so may be. I hope it doesn't hurt, I hope it's quick and over. But that's why that hasn't happened to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So now I'm out. When I had about three hundred yards to the end of the-- to-- to-- to get to the-- to the last obstacle. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And miraculously, all I needed to do was crawl under it and lift it up with my back to make sure that no body parts get caught in it-- in it, and that took me less than a minute.

speaker: interviewee
text: It didn't take me more than four minutes to get out of this camp, when one wouldn't think it could be done. And of course, it was tried off and on by the-- during the normal-- during normal circumstances when the camp was properly guarded. There were not all that many guards because everybody was dead except six or eight [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] carpenters were left. They saved them, including my friend that's now in Paris. He was one of those.

speaker: interviewer
text: Cobblers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Carpenters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Carpenters.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyway, the second I was under the last obstacle, the guns and the bullets were flying left and right and right and left. So they finally must have seen me that-- but it was 10 yards tall grass. And I went like this, this and back. And there's about 500 yards to the edge of the forest. I couldn't run that fast today but that took me about one and a quarter minute and I was in the forest. So they finally, finally saw me but was too late for them. And I said, I'm the only one in the world now, the only one in Europe, the only Jew that's still alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went into the forest, deep, and I walked all night. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When day broke, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I came to the grave. So what I did, I walked, and walked, and walked, I walked around in a circle. And I came to the grave where all the-- all the people that got shot the day before. And that grave was boiling. Pop, pop, pop, pop, pop, pop, pop.

speaker: interviewee
text: The blood. Many-- many of the people that got shot were not totally dead. Maybe they were unconscious and the hot blood and 500 people, one on top of the other. It was-- pop, pop. It was a sound, gruesome. It's-- it's spooky. So naturally, and later on I learned that two escaped that were shot from the grave. Two of the survivors that I know.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then I went in the other direction. And I was in the forest, and I tell you some of the episodes, what went on. Was in the forest for about three weeks. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- it was in summer. Blueberries were all over the forest. Forest strawberries were all over. So I sustained myself on that kind of food for the first two days. One day I get to an area, maybe 10, 15 feet across in the forest, which thick forest but there was an area where there-- there were no trees but 10 by 10, 15 by 15. And I see there-- there's a burlap sack back there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I look and I was gonna lift it up. There was a hole in the ground, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and there were a pair of swimming trunks on top of it. I lifted it up. And there must have been about six, eight pounds of meats. Sausage, kielbasa, and ham, and other sausage. No bread. Shoe polish, a carbine, bullets, a shaving kit and then that sack. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My god. It doesn't take long to put two and two together. Somebody is hiding out same like me. I go back into the forest, I look, I watch [INAUDIBLE] for about an hour. That's it. I went back, I said look, I said to myself, somebody's hiding like me. He wouldn't mind the broke off piece of-- of that sausage and I ate it. That's you talking about miracles from the sky. Then again, nobody showed and I stayed all night in the area. Finally, I took the whole thing, including the gun. Put everything in that knapsack. We left, go deeper into the forest. Now, I dropped the carbine. I said, I don't need it. Now as I said to myself, in case there's an encounter-- If I have a carbine, my chances is somebody would be scared. It's that-- you don't calculate that careful, but you make quick judgement and you react to it. It was also too heavy. And in those days, I didn't know that well how to run-- use one either. Afterwards I did. And the front comes closer. Then I walk, I see some bodies laying there, right there in between, you know, the-- the forest and there's a road right between another road. A man was laying there with his hair falling off from the heat and all this, and I saw another one. But you're so hardened, you don't-- it doesn't affect you. You know, like for example, I was in that barrack A, I told you. And we were lined up there, 25 on every layer, where under normal circumstances maybe only six could lay comfortably. We wake up in the morning, you know, and the guy next door to you is dead. You don't worry about him, just look what kind of shoes they have. They're better than you, or his jacket better than yours or whatever you have. You can't get emotional in things like that. I guess that's built in-- in to you-- to you. You got to be strong, otherwise you only worry about yourself then, basically. You worry about collectively but when it comes really down to-- to [INAUDIBLE], it's survival.

speaker: interviewee
text: So suddenly I get caught in a-- in a round up between the Germans and the Poles from that particular area. They were running away with the horses [INAUDIBLE], their cows and stuff like that. And I'm there, right there in the middle. And suddenly I get [INAUDIBLE] only see a few soldiers there. And I run like hell. And I push myself under a pile of small woods, you know, little-- little-- from-- from branches and stuff that are piled up. And then they run after me, they passed me right by. Didn't see me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Once again, one-- once again over those thousands of episodes that every minute, every second, every minute, every hour, and so on, as I mentioned several times before. But it's just a little link in that long, long, unbelievable story. There again, as I mentioned before to this young lady, reality is stranger than fiction.

speaker: interviewee
text: So a few days later, [? approach-- ?] I see Polish farmers, approached them, [INAUDIBLE]. I didn't realize how dangerous it is to co-mingle with them, even during that period. Because they give you to the Nazis in no time for whatever reason. For their own, because they don't like Jews, but anyway I took a chance.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I worked for them. And I went behind the horse, and I made haystacks, and I-- I pulled out the-- the [INAUDIBLE], you know, beets. And they gave me the-- the food, the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Noodles and milk.

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh? Yeah. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. Yup.

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh? In any case-- and a couple nights later, it was raining.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they did save you. They did give you food.

speaker: interviewee
text: They give me a something, but listen. A couple nights later, it was raining, miserable. And I crawled under the haystacks, [INAUDIBLE]. I messed up a bunch of haystacks. In my mind, I didn't. Next morning I went back to that group of farmers. He got so mad. When you upset the haystack, it-- it rots, you know, because the-- the grass goes a certain way. He was so mad, and he said, look what you did to my hay. I-- you better come here. The-- the-- the Germans are over there, I'll take you right to them. I heard that, I left. I disappeared. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I come to a group of partisans, the Armia Ludowa. There was the Armia Krajowa and Armia Ludowa. The Armia Ludowa means the people's army. Armia Krajowa was the army--

speaker: interviewer
text: Of the country?

speaker: interviewee
text: --from the exiled government in-- in London, which are worse than-- than the Ludowa, but none of them were good. But in any case. Then I found out they were Armia Krajowa. But I-- afterwards I found out. And I told them where I am, [? and this. ?] At the time, I still had my carbine. I'd say I'm a partisan too, [INAUDIBLE]. Well, they took me, and they wanted me to join them and stuff like that. I had a gut feeling that it's not good for me. Soon as I had an opportunity, I left the area. I was with them a day and a half. I said, it's too dangerous. All kinds of things. Later on, I heard the real horror stories what they did to the Jews. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So then I [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] went deeper into the forest. Suddenly there was a major dual between the Russians and the Germans soldiers. And the shrapnel, they were flying left and right. And one evening, I-- I get very close to the edge of the forest, and I see the German soldiers are digging and preparing for a major dual. I saw that, and I stepped back. They just got there, and they started setting up. There were just all set up, maybe an hour and a half later. They must have gotten a new order. Packed everything up and retreated. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But then a few minutes in, then I went I further. Then, my god, I'm in the forest. And the shrapnel is falling left and right, now you hear them hitting the trees [INAUDIBLE]. And-- and-- and the-- the-- the-- the-- the cannon bullets explode left and right and front and back of me. Bottom [? of ?] [? your ?] [? piece, ?] nothing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Finally-- another few intermediate little episodes, nothing major. But one morning about three weeks after I got home Treblinka, the edge of the forest in the other direction. There I see the Russian soldiers. Rows of them coming to this area. I said to myself, now I'm home free. But I said to myself, you know, the clothes that I had on. Some of it was-- it wasn't really a German uniform. But could be-- that's a military buttons and stuff. Forgot what jacket. I had it from the-- while I was still in the camp. And I didn't want to take a chance. So I get out of the forest and walk toward them, and I hold up my hands, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Which means?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Which means?

speaker: interviewee
text: You don't know that?

speaker: interviewer
text: I do but I want you to say it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Which means, comrade, don't shoot. I'm a Jewish individual. And they said, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. I'm not shooting. I went there. [INAUDIBLE] So naturally they have, there are the soldiers took me and said, let's go and see the [RUSSIAN]. [RUSSIAN], in-- in Russian, [RUSSIAN], which means the colonel. So now they interrogate me. And I say I come from the camp Treblinka. People didn't know yet at the time about Treblinka. There was a-- was a-- you know, Treblinka was the most easternly located camp. And Treblinka didn't mean anything. When I say, I was in this camp, and escaped, they killed so many, and so on and so forth. And then there were a lot of Jewish people in the Russian army. And there was a-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] a starchy-- starchy lieutenant, a Jewish lady. She said-- and the [NON-ENGLISH], he interrogated me. Give me a cigarette and this and that and talk to me. And she says, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. You lie. [NON-ENGLISH  SPEECH]. A real Dutch, a real German. I said, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. And don't ever ask me to verify it either, believe me. Which till now they ask me. But it-- they never ask, and I did. And I said-- I figured I'm going to take a chance. And I said in Russian, [SPEAKING RUSSIAN]. Which means, if you don't believe that I'm Jewish, can shoot me like a dog. He said, [SPEAKING RUSSIAN]. I said, [SPEAKING RUSSIAN]. I'm not sure that he would have shot me or not, but I took a chance because I was so sure, I'd say, I am!

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, couldn't you prove it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Didn't ask. I didn't tell and I didn't think. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They took me. They had a field [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] prison camp in the area if they capture the-- the Germans. In this case, it was a barn. Took me, put me right with the Nazis, with-- with the soldiers. Regular soldiers to now in prison. About 30, 40. To them, I wasn't any different. I'm a soldier. Talked to them German. I wouldn't tell him where I was. I didn't tell him that I'm just one of them. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Doesn't sound realistic but all so true and verified. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Every morning they interrogated me, and there was a [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], a Jewish lieutenant from Odessa. And I talk to him. He believed me. Said, I believe you and this and [INAUDIBLE] you're so [? strong ?] and did this and you've [INAUDIBLE] and this and that. This whole thing was all new to them, they have to go, they've got to go. Absolutely precise every day. They wrote books on me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Couldn't they ask you to pray for them? Show them how you prayed, show them that you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean Hebrew Shema Yisrael and all that stuff?

speaker: interviewer
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Meant nothing. But he was just a lowly officer. There he's got to get his orders. Then moved me from there from them to another camp by myself. One day, they interrogated me and there was a town nearby which is near Treblinka, in fact, Kossow, where meanwhile, some of the other Treblinka survivors gathered. Was a fellow by the name of Katz, Avrum Katz. I don't know how he found out that I'm being interrogated there. For some reason, he came there. He pointed out and-- who I am and what-- where we were, maybe meanwhile they learned. And they released me. And I went with the whole group and rejoined them in Kossow. And incidentally, but I'm going to talk about Kossow. Afterwards I learned there was a program in Kossow that killed 11 of the 22 Jews that congregated there.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's after Treblinka, even some Treblinka survivors. Can you believe that? And I'll tell you something-- well, you heard in Lublin, you heard all kinds of that stuff. OK, so wasn't Kossow. Then there were two groups. Was like competition. There was [INAUDIBLE]. She wanted people to work for them and make [NON-ENGLISH] You know what that is? That-- that's-- you know what [NON-ENGLISH] is? He understands. It's self-made liquor there. They had their own brewery. And then there was another one. There was a-- a family by the name of [? Koenigstein ?] that had their whole oh, [NON-ENGLISH] What's a [NON-ENGLISH]? What do you call a [NON-ENGLISH] in English? I only ever know it in-- in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Treasure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Treasure.

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Treasure.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not a treasure, but a [NON-ENGLISH] is also where there's a farm.

speaker: interviewer
text: A farm. That's what I, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, a farm. OK. A big farm. And I elected to go with them. And I helped, and I-- I-- I went behind the plow. And I worked with them there for about four weeks. The fed me and stuff like-- the war was still on. Then in October an order came, [NON-ENGLISH] all able young men have to rejoin the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which army is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is the Polish army under the direction of General [INAUDIBLE] You ever heard that name? [INAUDIBLE]? Got pictures with him too. Mind you, but I was not a Polish citizen, but I may as well. And I joined them. When they recruited me, I went to [INAUDIBLE], and I joined the Polish army. And I was in the Polish army for one year and one month and was discharged in December 1945. And I was on Warszawa Praga when we were waiting before the Germans, the Polacks, killed themselves. And we didn't come-- go to Warsaw to rescue them because they wanted to kill-- because they wanted direction of the Russian army. It was just a-- a Polish division. But to me, once again, I got wounded too. And I've got my shrapnel here. I was in-- in the-- in the hospital there for six weeks. And then as soon as I felt good, they put me-- now [? listen, ?] I was part of the division that took in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you actually took Warsaw from the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely.
